content.text: Freshness and completeness checks are automated quality controls applied to RAG source documents at ingestion time and on an ongoing basis to ensure that documents entering the vector index are both current and structurally adequate. Freshness checks verify that a document's last-modified timestamp falls within an acceptable age window relative to its document category — a regulatory procedure that has not been updated in 18 months may be unacceptably stale regardless of its content quality. Completeness checks verify that required metadata fields, minimum content length, and expected structural sections are present. Together, these two checks form the first line of defence against a knowledge base that is superficially populated but operationally unreliable.

Agentic AI systems retrieve and present information as authoritative responses. When the underlying document is 14 months old in a domain where quarterly updates are standard, the AI system is not disclosing that the information may be outdated — it presents it with the same confidence as a document updated yesterday. Users rarely see source timestamps in a conversational AI interface, so the burden of freshness enforcement must sit with the data pipeline, not with users.

Completeness is equally critical. A chunk derived from a document missing its author, effective date, or domain classification cannot be correctly attributed, correctly scoped, or correctly retired when the owning domain requests removal. Incomplete metadata is not just a quality problem — it is a governance problem that blocks traceability.
— configurable maximum-age thresholds per document category ensure that the knowledge base cannot contain documents older than the governance policy allows.
— completeness gates at ingestion prevent documents with missing metadata from entering the index, where they would be difficult to identify and govern retroactively.
— every check result is logged with the document identifier, check type, result, and timestamp, creating an immutable record that the pipeline was operating within its quality parameters.

— completeness checks can be extended to coverage checks that compare the set of indexed documents against the expected catalogue, detecting topics or domains that are under-represented in the knowledge base.

— documents that fail checks are moved to a quarantine queue with a steward notification rather than silently excluded, ensuring that failures generate remediable work items rather than invisible gaps.

— some document management systems update the modified-date on trivial changes (whitespace, metadata edits). A document may appear fresh by timestamp while its substantive content is unchanged from three years ago.

— different document categories have radically different acceptable ages. A one-size-fits-all freshness threshold either over-rejects stable reference documents or under-rejects frequently-updated operational procedures.
— requiring 100% field completeness will reject many legitimate documents if the source systems do not populate all metadata fields consistently. Thresholds typically need empirical calibration.
— computing a meaningful coverage gap requires a catalogue of expected documents, which itself must be maintained. If the expected catalogue is stale, the coverage check generates false alarms.

Freshness checks compare a document's canonical modified timestamp against a category-specific maximum age. Document categories and their maximum ages are stored in a configuration registry. When a document arrives at the ingestion pipeline, it is looked up by category and its modified timestamp is compared to 'now - max_age_days'. A document from the Finance domain with max_age = 30 days that was last modified 35 days ago fails the freshness check.

Completeness checks validate two things: field presence (a defined set of required metadata fields must be non-null and non-empty) and content adequacy (the document body must meet minimum token or character count thresholds to produce meaningful chunks). Some implementations also check structural completeness — for policy documents, the presence of required sections such as 'Effective Date', 'Scope', and 'Owner'.

Documents that pass both checks proceed to the chunking and embedding pipeline with quality stamps written to their metadata. Documents that fail any check are written to a quarantine store with a failure reason, and the assigned domain steward receives a notification. Continuous freshness monitoring runs nightly to detect documents in the index that have aged past their freshness threshold since they were indexed.

attrs.alt: Freshness and completeness check pipeline: source system to freshness check to completeness check to decision gate, pass path to vector index, fail path to quarantine queue, continuous monitoring below.

content.text: The two checks run in series. Freshness is checked first because stale documents are likely to fail even if their metadata is complete — checking completeness on a document that will be rejected for staleness wastes compute. The decision gate routes documents to ingestion or quarantine, with a parallel write to the audit log in both cases. Continuous monitoring runs asynchronously on the indexed document population rather than on new arrivals.
Any production RAG system where source documents have varying ages and the consumer of AI responses cannot independently assess document recency.
When the knowledge base draws from systems (SharePoint, Confluence, legacy intranets) that do not enforce document lifecycle policies and may accumulate years of unmaintained content.
When regulatory requirements (HIPAA, financial services regulations) impose maximum-age constraints on information used in AI-assisted decisions.
When the organisation has experienced incidents caused by outdated or incomplete grounding documents and needs a technical control to prevent recurrence.
— store document categories and their maximum age in days in a configuration store (e.g., a YAML file or database table). Example: regulatory = 30 days, policy = 90 days, reference = 180 days.
Define required metadata fields per category

— not all categories require the same fields. Regulatory documents may require an 'effective_date' field that reference documents do not. Store field requirements in the same configuration as age limits.

— implement 'check_freshness(doc, config)' and 'check_completeness(doc, config)' as pure functions that return a result object with a pass/fail flag and failure reasons. Keep them decoupled so they can be tested independently.

— implement a quarantine table or queue that stores rejected documents with their failure reasons, timestamps, and assigned steward. Stewards should have a self-service interface to review and remediate quarantined documents.

— a nightly job scans all indexed documents, computes their current age relative to their category threshold, and flags those that have aged past their limit since indexing. These are auto-suspended and added to the remediation queue.

# quality/coverage_gap.py
# Compare expected document catalogue against indexed documents

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class CoverageReport:
    domain: str
    expected_count: int
    indexed_count: int
    missing_doc_ids: list[str]
    coverage_fraction: float


def compute_coverage_gap(
    expected_catalogue: dict[str, list[str]],  # domain -> list of doc_ids
    indexed_doc_ids: set[str],
) -> list[CoverageReport]:
    """
    expected_catalogue: {"hr": ["doc-001", "doc-002", ...], ...}
    indexed_doc_ids: set of doc_ids currently in the vector index
    """
    reports = []
    for domain, expected_ids in expected_catalogue.items():
        expected_set = set(expected_ids)
        missing = sorted(expected_set - indexed_doc_ids)
        indexed_in_domain = expected_set & indexed_doc_ids
        fraction = len(indexed_in_domain) / len(expected_set) if expected_set else 1.0
        reports.append(CoverageReport(
            domain=domain,
            expected_count=len(expected_set),
            indexed_count=len(indexed_in_domain),
            missing_doc_ids=missing,
            coverage_fraction=fraction,
        ))
    return sorted(reports, key=lambda r: r.coverage_fraction)  # lowest coverage first

An insurance company built a RAG assistant to help underwriters answer questions about coverage guidelines and actuarial tables. The knowledge base was populated from a SharePoint library of over 3,000 documents accumulated over seven years. No freshness or completeness checks were applied at initial ingestion.

Within two months, underwriters reported that the assistant was citing coverage guidelines that had been superseded by regulatory changes 18 months earlier. An audit revealed that 34% of the ingested documents were more than two years old, and 12% were missing their 'effective_date' and 'regulatory_basis' metadata fields — both required for accurate coverage guidance.

The team retroactively applied freshness and completeness checks to the existing index. This immediately quarantined 1,100 documents (37% of the index). The remaining 1,900 documents were re-reviewed by domain stewards over three weeks. After remediation, the knowledge base contained 2,200 documents (some quarantined documents were updated and re-ingested). Underwriter confidence in AI responses increased from 2.8 to 4.1 on a 5-point scale, and the assistant was expanded to two additional business lines.
Using modified_at from the file system rather than the authoritative source
— file system timestamps change on copy, download, and sync operations. Always use the document management system's canonical last-modified field.
Applying a single freshness threshold to all documents
— a 30-day freshness threshold rejects stable legal glossaries that haven't changed in three years but are still accurate. Category-specific thresholds are essential.
— quarantining documents without a clear remediation workflow creates a growing backlog of blocked content. Stewards need a self-service interface to review, update, and re-submit.
Failing to check indexed documents for post-indexing staleness
— freshness must be checked both at ingestion and continuously on the indexed population. A document indexed 29 days ago under a 30-day threshold becomes stale tomorrow.
Setting minimum content length too low
— a minimum of 10 characters passes empty table-of-contents pages. Set minimum content length based on what constitutes a meaningful chunk for the embedding model — typically 80–150 tokens.
— never hard-code age limits or required fields in the checker code. Store them in a versioned configuration that can be updated without code deployment.
— a document that will fail freshness does not need completeness checking. Check in order of cheapest rejection first.
Log every check result to the audit store
— both passes and failures must be logged. Audit queries often need to prove that a document was in scope and passing checks at a specific point in time, not just that it was not rejected.
Alert stewards on quarantine, not just on expiry
— when a document enters quarantine, the domain steward should be notified immediately with the failure reason and a link to the remediation portal.
Build coverage gap reporting into the governance dashboard
— completeness checks on individual documents do not reveal gaps in domain coverage. A weekly coverage gap report comparing expected vs indexed document counts surfaces structural gaps.

Freshness and completeness checks are lightweight operations — timestamp comparison and field presence validation add under 5ms per document. The bottleneck is the nightly staleness scan over the full indexed population. For a 100,000-document index, scanning all metadata records and computing ages takes approximately 1–5 minutes depending on the vector store's metadata query performance. Partition the scan by domain or date range if response time is critical.

Coverage gap reporting is a set-difference operation between the expected catalogue and the indexed document set. This scales linearly with catalogue size and is typically the fastest batch operation in the quality pipeline.
Generates HTML check reports with pass/fail history
Scheduling nightly staleness and coverage scans
Event-driven freshness re-check on source change
Reduces lag between source update and check trigger
Catalogue of expected documents for coverage gap

The quarantine queue must be append-only from the checker process's perspective. The checker writes failure records but should not have delete permissions on the quarantine store — only stewards (via the remediation portal) should be able to close quarantine records after review. This prevents an attacker who compromises the checker process from silently clearing failure records.
— the same document checked twice should produce the same result. Use the document's canonical content hash as the check key to avoid duplicate quarantine records on re-runs.
— the check audit log should be write-only for the checker service and read-only for stewards and auditors. No process should have delete access.
— if the nightly staleness checker fails to run, stale documents accumulate undetected. Alert on missed runs with the same urgency as a threshold breach.
Freshness checks enforce category-specific maximum-age limits, preventing stale documents from entering or remaining in the vector index.
Completeness checks enforce required metadata fields and minimum content length at ingestion time, blocking ungovernable documents before they enter the index.
Both checks must be applied at ingestion time and continuously on the indexed population — ingestion-only checks miss documents that age past their threshold after indexing.
A quarantine queue with steward notifications converts check failures into remediable work items rather than invisible gaps.
Category-specific configuration (stored externally, not hard-coded) is essential — a single threshold for all document types is almost always wrong.
Coverage gap reports complement individual-document checks by detecting structural gaps where entire topics or domains are missing from the index.
